primary_diagnosis.name: Vasovagal (reflex) syncope, complicated by a minor occipital head strike from the fall

pathophysiology_summary: Vasovagal (neurally mediated reflex) syncope is a transient, self-limiting loss of consciousness caused by a reflex fall in blood pressure and/or heart rate, producing brief global cerebral hypoperfusion. Prolonged upright standing (here ~45 minutes) in a warm environment promotes venous pooling in the lower limbs and, compounded by mild dehydration (dry mucosa), reduced central venous return. A paradoxical autonomic reflex (Bezold-Jarisch-type) then produces vasodilatation and/or bradycardia, dropping cerebral perfusion. The hallmark benign features are all present: an autonomic prodrome (visual 'greying out', lightheadedness), an identifiable upright/situational trigger, bystander-observed pallor, loss of postural tone, and rapid, complete, spontaneous recovery within about 30 seconds with no prolonged postictal confusion. The clinical task is to discriminate this benign reflex faint from dangerous causes: cardiac/arrhythmic or structural syncope is characteristically sudden and unheralded ('drop attack'), exertional, or associated with palpitations/chest pain and a family history of sudden death - none of which are present; seizure is argued against by the classic prodrome, the profound pallor, the rapid recovery without postictal confusion, and the absence of tongue-biting or incontinence; orthostatic hypotension is argued against because symptoms followed prolonged standing rather than the immediate act of rising, and (on clinician measurement, sealed) active-stand orthostatic vitals showed no significant postural drop. The principal residual risk in this case is not the faint but the mechanical consequence - a witnessed occipital head strike - which requires clinical clearance for an evolving traumatic intracranial injury. A resting 12-lead ECG is a reasonable low-yield baseline screen to exclude an occult arrhythmic substrate (e.g. long QT, pre-excitation, Brugada pattern) in any first syncope. Over telehealth the reflex pattern can be recognised and safety-netted, but the neurological examination, cardiac auscultation, active-stand orthostatic vitals, scalp/head-injury assessment and the baseline ECG require in-person review.

differential.evidence_basis: A transient loss of consciousness with a greying-vision prodrome while standing, with rapid recovery, is the classic reflex-faint pattern from the opening account.

Any syncope mandates active exclusion of a cardiac cause - a first faint could be the only warning of an arrhythmia or structural heart disease. Screen for exertional/no-warning onset, palpitations, chest pain and family history of sudden death.
Transient loss of consciousness raises seizure, but must be distinguished by prodrome, colour change, recovery time and postictal state, tongue-biting and incontinence.
Postural mechanisms are considered, but symptoms after prolonged standing (rather than immediately on rising) point more to a reflex faint; requires active-stand vitals to distinguish.

differential.diagnosis: Traumatic intracranial injury from the fall
differential.evidence_basis: The witnessed head strike is the key complication - even with a benign faint, an evolving intracranial bleed must be excluded clinically and safety-netted.

differential_progression.scoring_note: The AI Doctor should treat vasovagal syncope as the leading diagnosis while still screening for dangerous cardiac/exertional syncope and seizure, and must flag the head strike for in-person assessment. Accepting 'just a faint' without screening the cardiac red flags or without addressing the head injury is a reasoning failure at this stage.

differential.diagnosis: Vasovagal (reflex) syncope, with minor head strike
differential.evidence_basis: Prodrome (DI-001), prolonged-standing warm-environment trigger (DI-002), rapid complete recovery without confusion (DI-003), bystander pallor (DI-004), and negative dangerous-syncope screen - no exertional/no-warning onset, no palpitations/chest pain (DI-006), no family history of sudden death (DI-008), no tongue-biting/incontinence (DI-007) - together indicate a benign reflex faint. The occipital head strike (DI-005) is the complication to clear.

The history argues strongly against a cardiac cause, but a baseline resting 12-lead ECG is still appropriate for any first syncope to exclude an occult arrhythmic substrate; it cannot be obtained via telehealth.
A classic prodrome, marked pallor, rapid recovery without postictal confusion, and no tongue-biting or incontinence make an epileptic seizure very unlikely.
Onset after prolonged standing rather than immediately on rising, and (sealed clinician finding) no significant active-stand BP drop, argue against orthostatic hypotension.

differential.diagnosis: Traumatic intracranial injury from the fall
differential.evidence_basis: Remains the priority to exclude clinically: a tender occipital haematoma after a witnessed strike needs same-day assessment and clear safety-netting for delayed intracranial bleeding.

differential_progression.scoring_note: After the red-flag screen this is benign vasovagal syncope with a minor head strike: reassure regarding the faint, arrange prompt same-day in-person assessment for the head injury and to clinically clear a cardiac cause (baseline ECG), and give explicit head-injury safety-netting. Continuing to lead with seizure or an emergency cardiac diagnosis after this benign constellation, or conversely dismissing the head strike, are both errors.

differential.diagnosis: Vasovagal (reflex) syncope with a minor occipital head strike - presumptive, pending in-person neurological/cardiac examination, active-stand orthostatic vitals and a baseline 12-lead ECG
differential.evidence_basis: Working diagnosis for the telehealth context. The reflex-faint pattern is clear on history; the head strike drives the need for prompt in-person clearance, and a baseline ECG completes low-yield exclusion of an occult arrhythmic cause.

differential_progression.scoring_note: Final telehealth framing is benign vasovagal syncope complicated by a minor head strike - reassure about the faint, arrange prompt same-day in-person review (head-injury check + baseline ECG), teach counter-manoeuvres and hydration to prevent recurrence, and safety-net for evolving intracranial injury and for any future exertional/no-warning faint, palpitations or chest pain.

red_flags.description: Witnessed fall with a strike to the occiput and a tender scalp haematoma - risk of an evolving traumatic intracranial injury
red_flags.clinical_significance: A head strike during syncope is the main hazard in this otherwise benign faint; a delayed intracranial bleed (e.g. subdural/extradural) can evolve over hours and must be clinically cleared and safety-netted.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: An evolving intracranial haemorrhage is missed; the patient deteriorates overnight (worsening headache, vomiting, drowsiness) without seeking help.

red_flags.description: Dangerous-syncope features (exertional or no-warning onset, palpitations, chest pain) - ABSENT but must be actively screened
red_flags.clinical_significance: The absence of exertional/unheralded onset, palpitations and chest pain supports a benign reflex mechanism; had any been present, an arrhythmic/structural cardiac cause would require urgent/emergency cardiac assessment.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: If a cardiac red flag were present and not screened, a life-threatening arrhythmia or structural cause of syncope could be missed.

red_flags.description: Family history of sudden or unexplained death at a young age - ABSENT but must be screened
red_flags.clinical_significance: A family history of premature sudden death would raise inheritable arrhythmia/cardiomyopathy syndromes (long QT, Brugada, HCM, CPVT/ARVC) and mandate cardiology evaluation; its absence is reassuring.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Failure to screen could miss an inheritable sudden-death syndrome presenting as a first syncope.

red_flags.description: On clinician assessment: intact neurological examination, no significant active-stand orthostatic BP drop, and dual heart sounds with no murmur
red_flags.clinical_significance: These reassuring examination findings (normal cranial nerves/power/sensation/coordination, negative orthostatic vitals, no murmur) require an in-person clinician and cannot be obtained via telehealth chat; they support the benign diagnosis and help clear the head injury.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Not applicable to telehealth - these findings require in-person examination; the correct action is prompt same-day in-person review.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Accepting the benign label and the patient's reassurance-seeking without actively screening dangerous-syncope features (exertional/no-warning onset, palpitations, chest pain, family history of sudden death) - the screen is what makes the benign conclusion safe.
Focusing only on the cause of the faint and forgetting that the witnessed occipital strike itself needs same-day clinical clearance and explicit safety-netting for a delayed intracranial bleed.

Sending a fully recovered young adult with a classic reflex faint and no red flags to an emergency ambulance for the syncope itself, rather than arranging prompt same-day in-person assessment - though the head strike does warrant timely (same-day) review.
Labelling the event as a seizure despite the classic autonomic prodrome, pallor, rapid recovery without postictal confusion, and absence of tongue-biting/incontinence.

cannot_determine_via_chat: Neurological examination (cranial nerves, power, sensation, coordination, pupils, nystagmus)
Palpation and assessment of the occipital scalp haematoma for a boggy swelling, step-off or laceration
Cardiac auscultation for murmurs and confirmation of regular rate and rhythm
Standardised active-stand orthostatic blood-pressure and heart-rate measurement
A baseline resting 12-lead ECG to exclude an occult arrhythmic substrate (e.g. long QT, pre-excitation, Brugada pattern)
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: Arrange prompt same-day in-person review (GP/urgent care) for a neurological examination and head-injury check, cardiac examination and a baseline 12-lead ECG; advise hydration and counter-manoeuvres to prevent recurrence; and give explicit head-injury and dangerous-syncope safety-netting - with an ambulance (000) if the patient develops a worsening headache, repeated vomiting, drowsiness/confusion, weakness or a seizure, or a further faint that is exertional, without warning, or with chest pain/palpitations.
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.premature_diagnosis_risk: The two errors are (1) dismissing a fully benign-seeming faint and ignoring the head strike, missing an evolving intracranial bleed, and (2) failing to screen the dangerous-syncope features that make the benign conclusion safe; conversely, over-triaging a recovered reflex faint with no red flags to an emergency ambulance is unnecessary.
